goal: Eliminate end-to-end batch wait. Stream micro-batches of ~50 symbols through persist and derived metrics AS scraping continues, so first data is available in ~5s instead of waiting for all 2400 symbols to finish scraping.

architecture_overview.current_flow: scrape ALL 2400 → persist ALL → fundamentals ALL → derive ALL (sequential stages, each waits for previous)
architecture_overview.target_flow: scrape batch(50) → push to persist_queue → persist batch(50) → push to metrics_queue → derive batch(50) — ALL OVERLAPPING with next batch being scraped

throughput_model.streaming: scrape(45s) + pipeline_drain(~2s) = ~47s end-to-end, BUT first 50 symbols have derived metrics by t=7s
throughput_model.key_win: First data available in 5-7s instead of 50s. Critical for semi-live API ambition.

asyncio_structure.description: Single asyncio.run() orchestrating the entire streaming pipeline. Three concurrent layers: producers (scraper pool workers), persist consumer, metrics consumer.

queues.name: symbol_queue
queues.description: Existing queue. Pool workers pull (symbol, n_candles) pairs from here.

queues.name: persist_queue
queues.description: NEW. Pool workers push completed micro-batch dicts here after fundamentals extraction. Persist consumer reads from this.
queues.maxsize_rationale: Backpressure: at most 4 batches (200 symbols) buffered. If persist is slower than scraping, workers naturally block on put().

queues.name: metrics_queue
queues.description: NEW. Persist consumer pushes symbol name lists here after OHLCV+fundamentals are committed to SQLite. Metrics consumer reads from this.
queues.maxsize_rationale: Same backpressure logic. Metrics are fast (~0.3s per batch of 50), so this rarely fills.

coroutines.name: _pool_worker (MODIFIED)
coroutines.description: Same batch multiplexing logic. After request_batch_multiplexed returns and fundamentals are extracted, pushes the processed batch dict to persist_queue instead of (or in addition to) accumulating in shared results dict.

coroutines.name: _persist_consumer (NEW)
coroutines.description: Async coroutine. Loops: pull batch from persist_queue → run persist_ohlcv_base() via run_in_executor (sync SQLite I/O in thread) → run persist_fundamentals_snapshot() via run_in_executor → push symbol list to metrics_queue. Stops when it receives None sentinel.
coroutines.threading: Uses loop.run_in_executor(None, persist_fn, batch, ctx) because persist functions do synchronous SQLite I/O that would block the event loop.

coroutines.name: _metrics_consumer (NEW)
coroutines.description: Async coroutine. Loops: pull symbol list from metrics_queue → run 3 derived metrics runners in PARALLEL via ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) → await all 3. Stops when it receives None sentinel.
coroutines.threading: Uses loop.run_in_executor(metrics_executor, runner_fn, symbols, ctx) for each of the 3 runners. The 3 runners execute concurrently in threads (same as current ThreadPoolExecutor pattern in main_runner.py).

lifecycle: 4. Launches pool workers (same _run_pool pattern but workers push to persist_queue)
6. await persist_queue.put(None) — signal persist consumer to stop
7. await persist_task — persist consumer drains queue and exits
8. await metrics_queue.put(None) — signal metrics consumer to stop
9. await metrics_task — metrics consumer drains queue and exits

file_changes.new_signature: def main(argv: Optional[List[str]] = None) -> int (signature unchanged, internals restructured)

changes.what: Replace sequential scrape→persist→fundamentals→metrics stages with single streaming pipeline call
changes.detail: The 4 sequential ctx.span() blocks (tv_websocket_scrape, ohlcv_persist, fundamentals_persist, derived_metrics) are replaced by a single ctx.span('streaming_pipeline') that calls asyncio.run(_streaming_pipeline(...))
changes.new_structure: Single call: all_batch_data = asyncio.run(_streaming_pipeline(plan, timeframe, ctx))

changes.detail: New async function that orchestrates persist_queue, metrics_queue, persist_consumer, metrics_consumer, and pool workers. Returns aggregated results dict.
changes.new_signature: async def _streaming_pipeline(plan: dict, timeframe: str, ctx: RunContext) -> dict
async def _persist_consumer(persist_queue: asyncio.Queue, metrics_queue: asyncio.Queue, ctx: RunContext) -> None
changes.detail: Loops: batch = await persist_queue.get() → run_in_executor(persist_ohlcv_base) → run_in_executor(persist_fundamentals_snapshot) → await metrics_queue.put(list(batch.keys())). Exits on None sentinel.

changes.new_signature: async def _metrics_consumer(metrics_queue: asyncio.Queue, ctx: RunContext) -> None
changes.detail: Loops: symbols = await metrics_queue.get() → run_in_executor(run_price_performance_1d, symbols) + run_in_executor(run_volatility_1d, symbols) + run_in_executor(run_volume_1d, symbols) via asyncio.gather. Exits on None sentinel.

changes.what: Move schema initialization BEFORE streaming pipeline
changes.detail: Currently, each derived metrics runner calls init_*_schema() on every invocation. With streaming, they're called ~48 times instead of once. Move init_volatility_schema(), init_performance_schema(), init_volume_schema() to run ONCE before _streaming_pipeline starts.
changes.rationale: Idempotent CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS is safe to call once. Saves 47 redundant schema checks.

file_changes.change_type: MODERATE — add persist_queue parameter to worker chain

changes.current_signature: async def _pool_worker(worker_id, queue, catalog, timeframe, ctx, results, failures, stage)
changes.new_signature: async def _pool_worker(worker_id, queue, catalog, timeframe, ctx, results, failures, stage, persist_queue)
changes.detail: After processing batch_results and extracting fundamentals, push the processed batch dict to persist_queue: await persist_queue.put(batch_processed). ALSO still populate results dict for final count/reconciliation.

changes.current_signature: async def _run_pool(plan, timeframe, catalog, ctx, stage) -> tuple
changes.new_signature: async def _run_pool(plan, timeframe, catalog, ctx, stage, persist_queue=None) -> tuple
changes.detail: Pass persist_queue through to _pool_worker. When persist_queue is None (backward compat), workers only populate results dict (current behavior).

changes.current_signature: def run_tv_websocket_scraper(plan, timeframe, ctx, *, stage) -> dict
changes.new_signature: def run_tv_websocket_scraper(plan, timeframe, ctx, *, stage, persist_queue=None) -> dict
changes.detail: Pass persist_queue through to _run_pool. Backward compatible: when None, behaves exactly as before.

Since main_runner will call asyncio.run() itself to run the streaming pipeline, it can call _run_pool_streaming directly instead of going through run_tv_websocket_scraper. This avoids the nested asyncio.run() problem (asyncio.run inside asyncio.run is illegal). The scraper module would export _run_pool (renamed to run_pool) as a public async function.
changes.rationale: Currently run_tv_websocket_scraper calls asyncio.run(_run_pool(...)). If main_runner also calls asyncio.run(), we can't nest them. The streaming pipeline needs ONE asyncio.run() at the top level.

file_changes.critical_note: The nested asyncio.run() problem is the #1 structural challenge. Currently: main_runner (sync) → run_tv_websocket_scraper (sync) → asyncio.run(_run_pool). For streaming: main_runner (sync) → asyncio.run(_streaming_pipeline) where _streaming_pipeline (async) → _run_pool (async) directly. This means run_tv_websocket_scraper's role changes from 'sync wrapper with asyncio.run()' to 'exports an async function that the top-level asyncio.run() calls directly'.

file_changes.change_type: MINOR — skip schema init when called in streaming mode
file_changes.new_signature: def run_volatility_1d(symbols: List[str], ctx=None, *, _schema_ready: bool = False) -> None

changes.what: Add _schema_ready flag to skip redundant schema init
changes.detail: When _schema_ready=True, skip the init_volatility_schema() call at the top. Main_runner calls init once before streaming starts, then passes _schema_ready=True for all micro-batch calls.
changes.rationale: Without this, init_volatility_schema() runs ~48 times (once per batch). It's idempotent but wasteful.

no_change_needed: SQL bulk read pattern — works identically for 50 or 2400 symbols
Pandas groupby vectorization — works identically for any N
Bulk upsert — works identically for any N
Connection management — creates and closes its own connection per call

file_changes.new_signature: def run_price_performance_1d(symbols: List[str], ctx=None, *, _schema_ready: bool = False) -> None

changes.what: Add _schema_ready flag to skip redundant schema init

file_changes.new_signature: def run_volume_1d(symbols: List[str], ctx=None, *, _schema_ready: bool = False) -> None

changes.what: Add _schema_ready flag to skip redundant schema init

file_changes.current_signature: def persist_ohlcv_base(batch_data: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]], ctx) -> None
file_changes.rationale: Already accepts batches of any size. Calendar cache is module-level (shared across calls). Works identically for 50 or 2400 symbols.

file_changes.current_signature: def persist_fundamentals_snapshot(all_batch_data: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]], ctx: RunContext) -> None
file_changes.rationale: Already accepts batches of any size. Single executemany call. Works identically for 50 or 2400 symbols.
Batch multiplexing, timeouts, per-session traces — all untouched. The streaming migration is purely orchestration-level.

concurrency_safety.sqlite_wal: All connections use PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL. WAL supports concurrent readers with one writer. Multiple micro-batch persists are serialized by the single _persist_consumer coroutine — only one persist call runs at a time.
concurrency_safety.persist_serialization: Critical: _persist_consumer processes batches ONE AT A TIME (sequential await). This means persist_ohlcv_base and persist_fundamentals_snapshot for batch N complete BEFORE batch N+1 starts. No concurrent writes to the same table.
concurrency_safety.metrics_parallelism: Within each micro-batch, the 3 derived metrics run in parallel threads (ThreadPoolExecutor(3)). They READ from tv_candles_raw (concurrent reads OK under WAL) and WRITE to different tables (performance_1d, volatility_1d, volume_1d). No write contention.
concurrency_safety.metrics_serialization: _metrics_consumer processes batches sequentially. Batch N's 3 metrics complete before batch N+1's start. So we never have 6+ concurrent metric threads — always exactly 3 (one per metric type).
concurrency_safety.results_dict_safety: The shared results dict is written by pool workers (asyncio coroutines on the same thread). asyncio is single-threaded — no data races between coroutines. No lock needed.
concurrency_safety.context_var_safety: RunContext.span() uses ContextVar which is safe across threads (each thread gets its own copy). The metrics ThreadPoolExecutor threads each see their own span stack, same as current architecture.

persist_failure.strategy: Log error via ctx.event, skip the batch (don't push to metrics_queue). Continue processing next batch. The symbols from the failed batch will have OHLCV data in the scraper results but no derived metrics.
persist_failure.rationale: A persist failure for one batch should not halt the entire pipeline. The data can be re-derived on the next run.

metrics_failure.strategy: asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) — collect exceptions without crashing. Log via ctx.event. Continue processing next batch.
metrics_failure.rationale: Same as persist failure. Metrics can be re-derived.

scraper_failure.strategy: Existing requeue logic (MAX_BATCH_RETRIES) is unchanged. Failed symbols go to failures list. Persist consumer never sees them.

consumer_crash.strategy: Wrap each consumer's while loop body in try/except. On exception, log and continue to next batch. Only exit on None sentinel or fatal error.
consumer_crash.rationale: One bad batch should not kill the consumer and starve all subsequent batches.

span_structure.current: 4 sequential spans: tv_websocket_scrape → ohlcv_persist → fundamentals_persist → derived_metrics
span_structure.new: 1 outer span: streaming_pipeline. Inner events track per-batch progress: batch_persisted(batch_n, symbols=50), batch_metrics_done(batch_n, symbols=50). Total duration is the streaming span.
span_structure.rationale: Sequential spans don't make sense when stages overlap. A single span with granular events provides the same visibility.

events: batch_metrics_done(batch_n, symbols, perf_ok, vol_ok, volume_ok, duration_s)

migration_steps.description: Add _schema_ready flag to all 3 derived metrics runners
Export _run_pool as public async function from tv_websocket_scraper.py. Rename to run_pool_async. Keep run_tv_websocket_scraper as backward-compatible sync wrapper.
migration_steps.risk: LOW — additive change, existing code path unchanged

migration_steps.description: Add persist_queue parameter to _pool_worker and run_pool_async. When persist_queue is not None, push completed batch dicts to it. Always populate results dict for final reconciliation.
migration_steps.risk: LOW — None default preserves existing behavior

migration_steps.description: Implement _streaming_pipeline, _persist_consumer, and _metrics_consumer in main_runner.py. Wire up the queues and lifecycle.
migration_steps.risk: MEDIUM — new orchestration logic, must get shutdown sequence right

migration_steps.description: Replace the 4 sequential stages in main() with a single streaming_pipeline call. Update span structure and observability events.
migration_steps.risk: MEDIUM — changes the execution model. Must validate with --dji (30 symbols) before full run.

migration_steps.description: Test with --dji (30 symbols, 1 batch). Verify: OHLCV persisted, fundamentals persisted, all 3 derived metrics computed, no data loss.
Test with --spx (500 symbols, 10 batches). Verify streaming overlap is working: check log timestamps to confirm persist starts before scraping finishes.

risks_and_mitigations.description: main_runner currently calls run_tv_websocket_scraper which calls asyncio.run(). If main_runner also calls asyncio.run() for streaming, we get RuntimeError('cannot be called from a running event loop').
risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Step 2 exports run_pool_async as a public async function. The streaming pipeline calls it directly (no nested asyncio.run). run_tv_websocket_scraper (sync wrapper) is kept for backward compat / CLI testing but NOT used in streaming mode.
risks_and_mitigations.severity: CRITICAL — will crash immediately if not handled

risks_and_mitigations.description: If metrics_consumer runs before persist_consumer has committed the OHLCV data for that batch, the SQL query will read old data.
risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Persist consumer pushes to metrics_queue ONLY AFTER conn.commit() returns. The SQLite WAL guarantees subsequent reads see committed data. The queue ordering guarantees metrics never run before persist completes for that batch.
risks_and_mitigations.severity: HIGH — would produce wrong results silently

risks_and_mitigations.description: If persist_queue fills up (maxsize=4), pool workers block on put(). This pauses scraping, which wastes the WebSocket connections.
risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Persist is fast (~0.08s per batch of 50). Scraping is slow (~5s per batch). The queue will never fill up in practice. maxsize=4 is conservative safety net. If it does fill, blocking is the correct behavior (backpressure).

risks_and_mitigations.description: If _persist_consumer crashes, no more batches get persisted. Pool workers keep scraping and pushing to the queue until it fills, then block forever.
risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Wrap consumer loop body in try/except. On per-batch failure, log and continue. For true crashes (memory, segfault), the entire asyncio.run() fails and main() catches it.

risks_and_mitigations.description: Current report has clean sequential stage durations. Streaming blurs stage boundaries.
risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Single streaming_pipeline span with per-batch events. Report includes: first_batch_persisted_at, last_batch_derived_at, total_duration. Users can still compute effective persist and metrics throughput from the events.
